coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of antibacterial fluorescent carbon dots and green coal-like hydrochar from tea Industry byproducts via hydrothermal carbonization

coredata.dc:creator: Duarah, Prangan
Debnath, Banhisikha
Purkait, Mihir Kumar
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an experimental framework employing hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) for the valorisation of factory tea waste as a solid fuel. Employing a centre composite design and response surface methodology, the investigation systematically assesses the influence of two critical process parameters, temperature (180–220 °C) and time (0–240 min), on higher heating value (HHV), hydrochar yield, and energy yield. The study identifies an optimized HTC condition (220°C, 55 min) resulting in the recorded HHV of 22.26±1.10 Mj/Kg. Combustion characteristics and kinetics of the factory tea waste-derived hydrochar (TW-hydrochar) is analysed through TGA and DTG, complemented by structural elucidation via FTIR and FE-SEM. Concurrently, the study reports the observation of carbon dots (TW-CDs) generation during TW-hydrochar production, characterized comprehensively through UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, XPS, Zeta potential, and fluorescence methods. Further, antimicrobial properties of TW-CDs are rigorously investigated against Bacillus subtilis and Escherichia coli, manifesting pronounced antibacterial activity against B. subtilis and E. coli as evidenced by a 12 mm and 11 mm inhibition zone, respectively. This research contributes to the scientific understanding of tea waste valorization through HTC, providing insights into cleaner fuel options and the emergence of carbon dots with potential antibacterial applications.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of antibacterial fluorescent carbon dots and green coal-like hydrochar from tea Industry byproducts via hydrothermal carbonization

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an experimental framework employing hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) for the valorisation of factory tea waste as a solid fuel. Employing a centre composite design and response surface methodology, the investigation systematically assesses the influence of two critical process parameters, temperature (180–220 °C) and time (0–240 min), on higher heating value (HHV), hydrochar yield, and energy yield. The study identifies an optimized HTC condition (220°C, 55 min) resulting in the recorded HHV of 22.26±1.10 Mj/Kg. Combustion characteristics and kinetics of the factory tea waste-derived hydrochar (TW-hydrochar) is analysed through TGA and DTG, complemented by structural elucidation via FTIR and FE-SEM. Concurrently, the study reports the observation of carbon dots (TW-CDs) generation during TW-hydrochar production, characterized comprehensively through UV–visible spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, XPS, Zeta potential, and fluorescence methods. Further, antimicrobial properties of TW-CDs are rigorously investigated against Bacillus subtilis and Escherichia coli, manifesting pronounced antibacterial activity against B. subtilis and E. coli as evidenced by a 12 mm and 11 mm inhibition zone, respectively. This research contributes to the scientific understanding of tea waste valorization through HTC, providing insights into cleaner fuel options and the emergence of carbon dots with potential antibacterial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tea, well-known for its global popularity, ranks second in terms of consumption, exceeded only by water. The remarkable health benefits of tea—which include anti-inflammatory, antidiabetic, anti-cancer, immuno-modulatory, cardiovascular-preventive, neuroprotective, and hepato-protective properties—are the primary cause of its rising popularity. The exponential growth in tea consumption has propelled tea production to unprecedented levels, with estimates projecting a rise from 6.3 million tonnes in 2020 to an anticipated 7.4 million tonnes by 2025 (Debnath et al., 2021; Duarah et al., 2023). The exponential growth in tea production has brought about a concurrent surge in the production of tea waste across multiple stages of tea production. This tea waste can be categorized based on its origin within the production process, namely i) waste originating in tea estates (tea seeds), ii) waste generated in tea factories during processing (Factory tea waste), and iii) Domestic tea waste (waste generated post-brewing, including spent tea leaves). Tea estates exhibit an annual yield of approximately 3–4 tons of tea seed per hectare. A substantial quantity of seeds is often rejected due to dormancy-related issues, leading to the wastage of a significant volume of seeds. Researchers have diligently addressed the valorization of these seeds over the past decade. Demirbas (2010) explored tea seed upgrading facilities and provided an economic assessment of biodiesel production derived from tea seed oil. Additionally, tea seeds have garnered attention as a potential source of bioactive compounds. Numerous methodologies have been developed by researchers to harness tea seeds for applications in the pharmaceutical and nutraceutical industries (Singh et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2023). Similarly, spent tea leaves and domestic tea waste have been extensively investigated for their potential to yield value-added products. Examples include the production of biochar and activated carbon, applicable in diverse environmental applications such as wastewater treatment, catalysis, and electrochemical energy storage (Guclu et al., 2021). However, it is noteworthy that the research addressing the conversion of factory tea waste (FTW) into value-added products is relatively limited to the best of our knowledge. Tea factories generate significant amounts of waste, including fibres, leaves, and dust, lacking proper management systems for treatment. In majority of the tea-producing countries, tea waste is underutilized, with only a small portion used for caffeine extraction. The disposal of FTW, which contains caffeine, poses environmental risks and affects tea quality in garden areas. Spent tea leaves from global tea consumption are often improperly discarded, contributing to environmental problems (Debnath et al., 2022). This overlooked waste, rich in lignocellulosic biomass with low ash content, holds potential as a solid fuel source.

One of the most promising approaches to transforming waste into energy is hydrothermal carbonization (HTC). This process is advantageous since it operates under gentle reaction conditions, requires less energy, and can be applied to a broad variety of raw materials (Funke and Ziegler, 2010; Wu et al., 2023c). Operational parameters typically involve temperatures between 180 °C and 220 °C, with varying residence times (Licursi et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2022, 2023b). HTC involves complex reactions, resulting in hydrochar, a coal-like solid product with advantageous properties. Preparing solid fuels from HTC of biomass and waste resources, including damaged coffee beans (Santos Santana et al., 2020), rice husk, coconut shell (Cheng et al., 2022), sludge (Wang et al., 2019), rice straw (Yu et al., 2023a), food waste (Wu et al., 2024), municipal waste (Liu et al., 2021) and other similar materials, has recently become the subject of extensive study. The combustion and thermodynamic characteristics of hydrochars vary depending on the synthesis conditions due to the physical and chemical interactions that occur during the HTC process. In order to investigate the possible applications of hydrochars, it is essential to comprehend these alterations with change in parameters. The influence of temperature and residence time on the HTC of forestry waste was explored by Liang et al. (2022). The outcomes of the study revealed that raising the temperature of the HTC reduces hydrochar yield while raising the content of gas and liquid products, improving fixed carbon content, and enhancing combustion efficiency. Similarly, Wang et al. (2020) performed HTC on corn stalks and concluded that longer residence times and higher HTC temperatures do not yield optimal performance for solid fuels (Wang et al., 2020). Furthermore, the characteristic features of hydrochar, including its physical and chemical properties, are impacted by the qualities of the raw material used and the parameters of the production procedure, such as temperature and residence time. Therefore, it is important to do a comprehensive analysis of the impacts of these variables on any specific raw material being examined (Sabio et al., 2016). To the best of our knowledge, there are no previous studies in the literature that have explored the use of FTW as a solid fuel by converting it into hydrochar through the HTC process.

The purpose of this research is to understand the impact of temperature and residence time on the conversion of FTW to hydrochar during the HTC. The optimization of the parameters is achieved using response surface methodology (RSM) analysis, which thoroughly assesses the individual and interactive effects of hydrothermal reaction temperature and residence time. The combustion characteristics of prepared hydrochar were probed by using a thermogravimetric analyser (TGA) and estimation of activation energy is done by using Differential Friedman Method (DFM), Ozawa-Flynn-Wall (OFW), Kissinger-Akahira-Sunose (KAS), Starink (STK), and Simplified Distributed activation energy model (DAEM). In addition to solid fuel applications, this study also explores the generation of carbon dots (CDs), a burgeoning category of carbon nanomaterials known for their unique properties, during the production of hydrochar. Several works have demonstrated the sustainable synthesis of CDs using natural resources or renewable biomass, highlighting the potential of CDs in diverse fields such as fluorescence detection, biological imaging, and plant nano-fertilizers. The fluorescence performance of CDs is significantly higher than that of black carbon powder, indicating the potential of CDs in optoelectronic applications. Furthermore, the photodynamic ability of CDs has been shown to inhibit the growth of hepatoma, suggesting their potential in cancer therapy (Jayakumar et al., 2024; Tiwari et al., 2024). The CDs synthesized (TW-CDs) were characterized using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, the TW-CDs have been proven anti-bacterial against Bacillus subtilis and Escherichia coli.

The FTW used in this study was obtained from Arin Tea Pvt. Ltd. in Assam, India. It may be characterized as the byproduct of tea production, consisting of residual tea stems, leaves, and other plant material left over after the processing of tea leaves for commercial tea production. This waste material may include spent tea leaves, twigs, and other organic matter that may be generated throughout the many phases of tea production, including withering, rolling, oxidation, and drying. Once retrieved from the factory, the FTW underwent a meticulous cleansing process to eliminate all traces of dirt or soil particles, followed by sun-drying. The desiccated biomass was kept in a BOPP bag at ambient temperature for subsequent use in future studies. Milli-Q water was used as the solvent throughout all stages of the experimental procedures, including solution preparation.

A central composite spherical design in Design Expert (Version 7.0.0) to construct the tests for the theoretical model was utilized. This allowed the study to explore the aggregate effect of independent factors on the predicted responses or dependent variables. Preliminary studies using the existing literature established the minimum and maximum permissible limits for the chosen process parameters. By limiting the allowable residence time, the time required to produce hydrochar was reduced. Furthermore, the temperature limit that was determined corresponds to the point at which the majority of hydrochar production was reported.

Through the usage of a face-central composite design of trials, the range for the independent variable was established. The range was ascertained by considering an alpha value of 1.0 and the upper and lower limits of the process parameters. The summary of the experimental design, including the upper and lower limits for each independent variable, is presented in Table S1. In a CCD-based RSM, points beyond the normal range of variables are determined by using alpha values. These alpha values define the distance from the center of the design. By setting these distances, the upper and lower limits for the process parameters are determined, represented as +1 and −1 levels (Duarah et al., 2024). This strategy facilitates the comprehension of the impact of factors across a wider range of variables. In this study, the RSM model was employed to understand the impact of temperature and residence time on the hydrochar yield, energy yield, and higher heating value (HHV). Following the acquiring of all the response data and the subsequent fitness of relevant models are done using the design expert software. Following the collection of all response data, the subsequent fitting of relevant models was conducted using Design Expert software. The optimal alternative model has been found based on the p-value (the least desirable value) and the F-value (the most desirable value). The statistical analysis application was used to acquire both the p and F-values.

The elemental compositions of hydrochars were analyzed using an Automatic Elemental Analyzer (Elementar, vario MACRO cube) to quantify the carbon (C), hydrogen (H), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S) content. The energy content of hydrochars prepared at various temperature and residence time was determined through the HHV assessment using a Digital Bomb Calorimeter - Manual System CC01/M2 (Toshniwal Technologies Pvt. Limited). The hydrochars were subjected to combustion at a temperature of 750 °C for a duration of 1 h in a muffle furnace in order to determine the amount of ash content. Further, the oxygen content was calculated using Eq. (1) (Nzediegwu et al., 2022):
(1)

Additionally, the physicochemical properties of the hydrochar derived from FTW (TW-Hydrochar) prepared under optimized conditions were analyzed. The assessment of functional groups in hydrochar was performed by using a Shimadzu IR Affinity-1 instrument. The apparatus features a ceramic infrared (IR) source. ATR-FTIR spectra were recorded over the span of 4000–400 cm−1, with a 0.5 cm−1 maximum resolution. To assess combustion characteristics, hydrochar underwent analysis using a thermogravimetric analyzer (Model No: TG 209 F1 Libra; Make: M/s Netzsch, Germany). Thermogravimetry (TG) was used to monitor weight loss over time, while derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) was used to calculate the rate of weight loss at different temperatures. The experiments were conducted in an air atmosphere with a continuous flow, using a heating rate of 10 °C/min, throughout a temperature range of 30–900 °C. In order to reduce disruptions produced by air movement, a consistent flow of air at a rate of 10 mL/min was maintained throughout the experiments.

The hydrochar yield, ED (energy densification) and EY (energy yield) are determined by the following equations (Wang et al., 2022b).

Differential Friedman (DFM), Kissinger-Akahira-Sunose (KAS), Ozawa-Flynn-Wall (OFW), Starink (STK), and the Simplified Distributed activation energy model (DAEM) were explored to assess the combustion kinetics of the TW-hydrochar. ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of carbon dots during hydrochar production
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of hydrochar and FTW-derived Carbon dots (TW-CDs) are elucidated in Fig. 1 . Hydrochar was synthesized using tea waste sourced from a tea processing factory, characterized by its abundance in carbon content, widespread availability, and customary disposal as biomass.

The hydrothermal Teflon autoclave was employed as the reactor vessel for conducting the reactions at varying temperatures and retention times at a constant ratio of solid to water of 1:30. Subsequently, the samples were allowed to undergo natural cooling to attain ambient temperature conditions, followed by filtration utilizing deionized water. Centrifugation was then applied to the resulting solution for 20 min at 8000 revolutions per minute. The resultant solution was subsequently filtered, and the filtrate was collected and preserved for subsequent characterization (Feng et al., 2024). The solid hydrochar produced during the process was gathered and subsequently subjected to heating within a hot air oven for a period of 24 h. After the drying process, the mass of the sample was precisely measured and conserved for further analytical assessments.

Transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Model: 2100 F) was used to characterise the particle size and structure of the synthesised TW-CDs. A drop of TW-CDs solution was deposited on carbon-coated copper grids for TEM analysis, and the grids were allowed to oven dry before being examined. Image J software (version 1.47) was employed for TEM image analysis. The functional groups of FTW and TW-CDs were investigated using a Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Shimadzu IR Affinity-1). ATR-FTIR Spectra were recorded with the help of a ceramic infrared (IR) source embedded in the device, within the 4000–400 cm−1 scanning range with 0.5 cm−1 maximum resolution. Chemical bonding characteristics were elucidated through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (M/s Physical Electronics, USA; Model: PHI 5000 VersaProbe III). Liquid TW-CD extracts were oven-dried and kept in a desiccator for analysis. The TW-CDs in liquid form were coated on a small glass slide (12 mm × 6 mm × 1 mm) and dried at 50 °C in a hot air oven to form a thick layer before analysis. Crystallinity analysis was conducted using X-ray powder diffractometry (Rigaku Technologies, JAPAN) with Cu-Kαl radiation (λ = 1.5406Ǻ). Scanning was performed at a rate of 6°/min, with a diffraction angle ranging from 4° to 60° at ambient temperature. Similar to XPS analysis, the XRD sample preparation involved coating TW-CDs on a glass slide (75 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm) and drying in a hot air oven at 50 °C. A UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-1900, Shimadzu) was used to acquire the UV–visible absorption spectra of the TW-CDs. Fluorescence measurements were conducted with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4700, Hitachi). Surface charges of the synthesized TW-CDs were determined using an Electrophoretic Light Scattering (ELS) measuring device (Litesizer DLS 500, Anton Paar).

Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were chosen as Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial models, respectively, for an investigation into the antibacterial efficacy of TW-CDs. Both the microdilution broth technique and the agar well diffusion assay were applied in the assessment process. The bacterial strains underwent an initial culture phase in nutritional broth, maintained at 37 °C for a duration of 12 h. Subsequently, 100 μL aliquots of the resulting bacterial suspensions were uniformly distributed onto Nutrient agar plates. Wells with a consistent diameter of around 7 mm were meticulously established on the agar plates. Following this, 100 μL of TW-CDs suspensions, with a concentration of 400 μg/mL, were precisely introduced into each well. The plates were then incubated at 35 °C for 24 h. The resulting inhibition zones around the wells were measured using a calliper, and the experiments were conducted in triplicate.

The antibacterial activity of the microdilution broth method was evaluated by measuring the MICs (minimum inhibitory concentrations) of the bacteria in 96-well plates. The TW-CDs were initially concentrated at 100 % and then diluted from 50 % to 1.25 %. Subsequently, 100 µL of the bacterial solution was added to each well, except for the negative control (media only), and the plates were placed in an incubator at a temperature of 37 °C for a duration of 24 h. After the samples were incubated, 10 µL of each sample was evenly distributed on agar plates. The plates were then incubated at a temperature of 37 °C for an additional 24 h. The inhibition of bacterial growth was observed and recorded as the MIC. All experiments were performed in triplicate for accuracy and reliability (Bayram Sarıipek, 2024; Karan et al., 2024).

The experimental design carried out through Design Expert software (Version 7.0.0), involved 13 experiments focusing on the influential factors of reaction temperature and time. Responses, namely hydrochar yield, HHV, and energy yield, were measured. Detailed experimental conditions and response data are provided in Table S2. Table 1 presents the analysis of variance (ANOVA) findings for the three responses, providing insight into the significance of temperature and time as variables that influence hydrochar yield, HHV, and energy yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CCD-RSM-based process optimization for hydrochar production
ce:section.ce:para: The ANOVA analysis for TW-hydrochar yield, HHV, and energy yield, as presented in Table 1, provides comprehensive insights into the significance of various factors influencing these parameters. For TW-hydrochar yield, the overall model is highly significant (p < 0.0001), with temperature (A) and time (B) emerging as significant contributors (p = 0.0086 and p < 0.0001, respectively). This highlights the critical role of these factors in optimizing production conditions. The Lack of Fit analysis shows a non-significant result (p = 0.2567), indicating that the model adequately captures the observed data patterns. Similarly, for HHV, the overall model is significant (p = 0.0019), with temperature (p = 0.0048) and time (p = 0.0006) significantly influencing HHV. However, the interaction term (AB) and squared terms (A² and B²) are not significant (p > 0.05). The borderline significant Lack of Fit result (p = 0.0500) suggests the model may need further refinement. In the case of energy yield, the overall model shows high significance (p = 0.0002), with time (p < 0.0001) being a crucial factor, while temperature and other terms are not significant (p > 0.05). A significant Lack of Fit result (p = 0.0284) indicates the necessity for model improvement to enhance predictive accuracy. These findings underscore the importance of time and temperature in influencing hydrochar properties.

Fig. 2 (a-c) shows hyperbolic-shaped 3D surface graphs depicting the influence of temperature and holding time on hydrochar yield, HHV, and energy yield, respectively. The Eq (14) serves as a predictive model for TW-hydrochar yield during the preparation process.
(4)
Hydrochar Yield = + 38.35 − 2.18 A − 10.47 B + 0.11 AB + 0.16

The equation encapsulates a comprehensive understanding of the interplay between temperature, holding time, and their combined effects on TW-hydrochar yield. The HTC of biomass components initiates at relatively low temperatures, leading to a notable TW-hydrochar yield. At elevated temperatures exceeding 200 °C, lignocellulosic materials undergo hydrolysis, triggering the breakdown of molecular bonds and the formation of intermediate components with heightened reactivity (Elaigwu and Greenway, 2016). This catalyzes polycondensation reactions, consequently, enhancing the degree of carbonization within the solid product. Moreover, higher temperatures promote the rapid hydrolysis of additional biomass constituents into liquid or gaseous byproducts, which concurrently diminishes the solid phase content (Bhatt et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 2 (a), This investigation demonstrated that elevating the HTC temperature from 180 °C to 220 °C resulted in a reduction of the hydrochar yield.

Eq. (15) and Eq . (16) establish a fitting model for the HHV and energy yield in the context of hydrochar preparation.
(15)
HHV = 24.25 + 0.83 A + 1.21 B + 0.14 AB − 0.19

Initially, as illustrated in Fig. 2 (b), increases in temperature or residence time positively affect the HHV in the TW-hydrochar preparation process. However, as these factors continue to rise, their positive effects may diminish. Conversely, initial increases in temperature or residence time negatively impact energy yield, as shown in Fig. 2 (c), but there may be an optimal point where further increases become beneficial. Both equations incorporate linear and non-linear terms, highlighting the complex relationship between temperature, holding time, and their respective impacts on HHV and energy yield in hydrochar preparation.

Determining optimal process parameters in HTC requires careful balancing of temperature and residence time to maximize yield and HHV. Utilizing desirability as a metric, a numerical optimization technique was employed to identify the best conditions within predefined limits. The RSM-CCD technique identified an optimized condition of 220 °C and 53.45 min, achieving an HHV of 23.27 MJ/kg and a TW-hydrochar yield of 48.482 %. For the practical laboratory implementation, the temperature and the time considered are 220 °C and 55 min. Practical experiments validated these conditions, yielding 22.26±1.10 MJ/kg HHV and 45.85±1.22 % hydrochar. This optimization enhances HTC efficiency, resource utilization, and sustainability in industrial operations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TW-hydrochar produced through the optimized parameters
Degree of coalification and Physicochemical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of biofuel performance depends significantly on the HHV, a parameter that serves as an indicator of the degree to which biomass has undergone enhancement through HTC pretreatment. At the optimized condition of 220 °C for 55 min, the HHV of the resulting hydrochar (TW-hydrochar) was measured to be 22.26±1.10 MJ/kg, a value comparable to the HHV of conventional lignite coal. Moreover, Table 2 compares HHV values for hydrochar and biochar from various lignocellulosic biomass sources, showing that the HHV of TW-hydrochar (22.26 MJ/kg) is similar to those of other biomass-derived hydrochars, such as Virginia mallow (21.729 MJ/kg), woodsaw dust (20 MJ/kg) and olive pomace (25.56 MJ/kg), demonstrating its potential as a competitive biofuel.

Elevated temperatures were found to perform a crucial role in promoting the dehydration and decarboxylation processes within the hydrochar. This is corroborated by the data presented in Table 3, which illustrates a concurrent decrease in hydrogen (H) and oxygen (O) content, coupled with an increase in Carbon (C) content. This compositional shift contributed significantly to the substantial augmentation of the HHV, ultimately leading to the formation of hydrochar that possesses characteristics similar to black coal, thereby manifesting as a solid fuel with enhanced energy content. The CHNS analysis results for hydrochar prepared at different temperatures and residence times reveal significant variations in its composition. The nitrogen content in the hydrochar ranges from 2.89 % to 1.69 %, while the carbon content increases from 45.98 % to 56.08 % as temperature and residence time rise. Hydrogen and oxygen content fluctuate between 5.44 % and 6.25 % and 28.14–33.46 %, respectively. The sulfur content varies from 3.44 % to 5.70 %. As temperature and hold time increase, the atomic ratios of H/C and O/C decrease, indicating a shift towards a more carbon-rich and stable hydrochar product. Lower H/C and O/C ratios of fuel were generally deemed suitable due to lower energy loss, reduced emissions of water vapor, and less soot formation during combustion (Fallah et al., 2023). Further, the elemental analysis in Table 4 compares the chemical composition of FTW and TW-hydrochar produced at optimized conditions (220 °C for 55 min). The hydrochar shows a reduction in nitrogen (2.65–1.89 %) and oxygen (31.96–27.6 %) content, while carbon content increases significantly (44.84–55.08 %). Hydrogen content slightly decrease from 6.49 % to 5.82 %, and sulfur content increases from 3.915 % to 5.301 %. The ash content in hydrochar decreases substantially from 10.15 % to 4.3 %, indicating a purer carbon material. The H/C ratio decreases from 1.73 to 1.27, and the O/C ratio drops from 0.53 to 0.37, reflecting a more condensed and stable carbon structure.

The Van Krevelen diagram is a powerful tool for understanding the impact of HTC on the elemental compositions of raw-TW and TW-hydrochar. Fig. 3 (a) provides a visual representation coalification degree in terms of the atomic ratios of H/C and O/C, allowing for the assessment of changes in the chemical composition of the materials. The reduction in H/C and O/C ratios of the TW-hydrochar after HTC at 220 °C is substantiated by the Van Krevelen diagram, affirming a noteworthy influence on the elemental compositions. The observed alterations in the H/C and O/C ratios in the Van Krevelen diagram align with the chemical conversions that take place during HTC, such as dehydration, decarboxylation, and the elimination of oxygen-containing functional groups. These changes demonstrate the thermal and chemical processes that occur during HTC, resulting in the creation of hydrochar with modified elemental compositions and chemical functionalities (Nguyen et al., 2023).

The FTIR peaks identified in Fig. 3 (b) at 3336, 2926, 1611, and 1051 cm−1 for comparable samples offer important insights into the functional groups of the TW-hydrochar. The presence of hydroxyl groups are generally indicated by the stretching vibration of O-H bonds, which is demonstrated by the peak at 3336 cm−1. The peak at 2926 cm−1 is determined to be the result of the stretching vibration of C-H bonds in alkanes, which suggests the existence of organic molecules with saturated hydrocarbon chains. The stretching vibration of CC bonds, which is indicative of aromatic compounds like lignin or other aromatic structures, is shown by the peak at 1611 cm−1. Lastly, the C-O bond stretching vibration peak at 1051 cm−1 suggests alcohols, phenols, and additional compounds may present in the samples (Debnath et al., 2023). These FTIR peaks provide insights into the chemical functionalities present in the samples, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl groups, alkanes, aromatic compounds, and oxygen-containing functional groups.

The HTC of FTW resulted in the formation of TW-hydrochar characterized by an evident black coloration. This color change can be attributed to the complex chemical transformations occurring during the HTC process. Specifically, the dehydration, decarboxylation, and aromatization reactions contribute to the formation of carbon-rich aromatic structures, which impart the black color to the TW-hydrochar. Additionally, the Maillard reaction, which occurs between amino acids and reducing sugars present in the biomass, further enhances the darkening effect by producing melanin-like compounds. This multifaceted process involving both general chemical reactions and the Maillard reaction collectively leads to the observed black coloration of the hydrochar (Xu et al., 2018). To provide insights into the structural alterations at the microscale, a comprehensive examination was conducted utilizing FE-SEM for both the raw tea waste and the TW-hydrochar samples. The micrograph of the FTW (Fig. 3 (c)) revealed a smooth, flat, and large block structure. After hydrothermal treatment, the TW-hydrochar showed increased fragmentation and the formation of microspheres on its surface (Fig. 3 (d)). These microspheres result from the decomposition of lignin and cellulose. During HTC, lignin fragments decompose into phenolics and form phenolic hydrochar, which can be located on the surface of non-dissolved lignin or polyaromatic char. Simultaneously, cellulose retains its fiber skeleton, leading to microsphere production. The release of volatile substances during HTC also contributes to a rough and brittle surface texture in the hydrochar (Shen et al., 2017).

The combustion characteristics of TW-hydrochar were studied by the non-isothermal thermogravimetric analyzer. Fig. 4 (a) and (b) present the combustion TG-DTG profiles of TW-hydrochar at different heating rates (10, 20, and 30 °C/min). Regardless of the heating rate, the combustion conversion curves exhibit a consistent pattern; nevertheless, the decomposition temperatures increase with increasing heating rates. The observed TG profiles are consistent with the typical combustion patterns that have been described in biomass fuels. These patterns are essentially defined by three stages: dehydration, devolatilization, and char oxidation. There was an initial mass loss for all of the samples between ambient temperature and around 130 °C, and that was because the remaining water in TW-hydrochar evaporated (stage I). The second stage, devolatilization, followed, with temperatures ranging from 150 to 350 °C. The third stage involves the combustion of fixed carbon, which is associated with lignin degradation at temperatures between 400 and 600 °C. During the HTC process, water eliminates a portion of the loosely bound amorphous cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin through recondensation processes. This leaves behind mostly tightly bound cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, as well as other water-soluble extractives and intermediate products. The outcome of these complex reactions is solid hydrochar (Islam et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2023b). From the DTG curves presented in Fig. 4 (b), it was observed that there were two visible peaks in TW-hydrochar, which was attributed to the rich volatile matter and fixed carbon content. Both the TGA and DTG profiles indicate that when the samples are subjected to increased heating rates, a minor shift occurs and the peak temperature at which weight loss occurs increases. The observed thermal lag was attributed to the phenomena of heat transfer restriction, which results in a minor displacement of the peak temperature of weight loss when the samples are subjected to greater heating rates. With an increase in the heating rate, the temperature difference between the inside and outside of the particles also increases, leading to a reduced level of reactivity within the particles (Cardarelli and Barbanera, 2024).

TW-hydrochar combustion kinetics were investigated by means of five models: DFM, KAS, OFW, STK, and DAEM. Table 5 displays the activation energy (Ea), pre-exponential factor (A), and correlation coefficient (R²) for different degrees of conversion (α) for each model. The correlation between the kinetic models and experimental data was poor (R2 < 0.8) when the conversion value exceeded 0.8 (α > 0.8). When the conversion rates are high (α = 0.9), the kinetic data may exhibit abnormalities due to the intricate structure of the combustion process at this particular stage (Komandur et al., 2022). Based on the results attained using all the methods, the conversion range was restricted from 0.1 to 0.8 with an interval of 0.1. The average R² values for DFM, KAS, STK, OFW, and DAEM are all in the range of 0.98–0.99 for conversion rates 0.1–0.8, which demonstrates that the models accurately describe the combustion kinetics of the TW-hydrochar. This high R² indicates reliable kinetic data, allowing for the optimization of combustion processes for TW-hydrochar as a sustainable biofuel.

Regardless of the level of conversion, there is a similar pattern in the values of Ea derived from different models. For instance, when the conversions are lower (α = 0.1–0.4), the Ea for DFM, OFW, KAS, STK, and DAEM are 75.16–100.06 kJ/mol, 80.79–91.81 kJ/mol, 75.26–85.95 kJ/mol 75.59–86.30, kJ/mol, and75.26 to 87.00 kJ/mol, respectively. The combustion process becomes more energy-intensive as the conversion increases, as seen by the considerable rise in the Ea. For higher conversions (α = 0.8), the Ea values escalate to 210.37 kJ/mol (DFM), 177.25 kJ/mol (KAS), 179.85 kJ/mol (OFW), 177.58 kJ/mol (STK), and 177.25 kJ/mol (DAEM). This increase suggests that the remaining hydrochar becomes more resistant to combustion as the material decomposes, requiring higher energy input for further breakdown. The pre-exponential factor (A), which indicates the frequency of successful collisions leading to combustion, also varies significantly. The average values for A across the models are high, particularly for DFM (5.77 ×1013 min⁻¹), indicating a high reaction rate. The other models also show high values: 1.25 ×1012 min⁻¹ (KAS), 1.02 ×1010 min⁻¹ (OFW), 1.03 ×1012 min⁻¹ (STK), and 7.76 ×1011 min⁻¹ (DAEM), reflecting the complexity and rapidity of the combustion reactions at different stages of conversion.

The reported TW-hydrochar in this study exhibited average Ea ranging from 102.87 to 127.55 kJ/mol, which is comparable to hydrochar derived from sewage sludge (∼130 kJ/mol) (Wilk et al., 2021), municipal sludge (130–140 kJ/mol) (Liu et al., 2023), wood sawdust (171.7 kJ/mol) (Kabakcı, 2020), agricultural waste (99.5) (Wu et al., 2023a). The resulting Ea for TW-hydrochar also indicates that its combustion process aligns with the typical Ea range observed for coal (80–170 kJ/mol) (Wang et al., 2022a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TW-CDs generated during the production of hydrochar

ce:section.ce:para: The properties of the TW-CD generated during the production of TW-hydrochar were analyzed using various standard analytical methods. The optical properties of TW-CDs were examined for potential applications. The solution exhibited a light brown color and demonstrated blue fluorescence under UV light ( Fig. 5 (a)). The UV–VIS absorption spectrum (Fig. 5 (a)) indicated a peak at 248 nm (π-π* transition of CC bonds) and peaks at 270 and 327 nm (n-π* transition of C-O bonds) (Zhang et al., 2021). Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra revealed maximum wavelengths at 420 nm (λex) and 512 nm (λem), respectively. Emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths (320–500 nm) showed in Fig. 5 (b) represented an excitation-dependent, adjustable emission spectrum, with peaks red-shifting from 470 to 550 nm as excitation increased from 320 to 500 nm, accompanied by intensity reduction. The XRD pattern of the synthesized TW-CDs is shown in Fig. 5 (c). The broad and diffused hump-like diffraction peak appearing at 2θ=22.24° indicates the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms of amorphous structure in the quantum dots (Gunjal et al., 2019; Inayat et al., 2023; Park et al., 2023). Fig. 5 (d) shows the FTIR spectrum of the tea waste-derived carbon dots. The IR spectrum reveals that the surface of TW-CDs contains many hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups. The broad peak located at around 3375 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl (O−H) groups present on the TW-CDs surface (Huang et al., 2022; Punyasamudram et al., 2023). The peak near 2121 cm−1 is attributed to C−N stretching vibration. The strong absorption band appearing at 1648 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of CC, CO and CN bonds (Oliveira et al., 2023; Qandeel et al., 2023). The peak at 1557 cm−1 represents the N−O group and at 1414 cm−1 corresponds to C−N stretching vibration. The IR bands at 1356 and 1023 cm−1 are related to C−O−C asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetrical stretching vibration (Bu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2022; Punyasamudram et al., 2023). The existence of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the carbon cores of the prepared quantum dots is the reason behind their good dispersion and high solubility in water (Tony Elizabeth et al., 2023). The morphology of the dots was analyzed by TEM. The TEM images collected are shown in Fig. 5 (e) and (f). The prepared sample of TW-CDs manifests a homogenous distribution of discrete entities, presenting spherical morphology. The entities have an average diameter of around 6 nm.

Fig. 6 (a) depicts XPS spectra obtained from survey scans, confirming the composition of TW-CDs. The spectra reveal the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N). Notably, strong signals corresponding to the C1 s and O1 s peaks are observed at binding energies of 284.33 eV and 532.33 eV, respectively. Additionally, weaker signals for N1 s and Okkl are discerned near 400 eV and 1000 eV, respectively (Dhara et al., 2023). The deconvoluted XPS spectra of the prepared TW-CDs revealed significant peaks in the C1 s spectrum (Fig. 6 (b)) at 284.68, 286, and 288.03 eV, corresponding to sp2C–C/CC, C-O, and O-C = O/OC-NH, respectively (Barman et al., 2014). Additionally, the deconvoluted O1 s spectrum (Fig. 6 (c)) for the CDs suggested the presence of organic C-O bonds at 532.79 eV, with additional peaks at 531.26 and 532.31 eV attributed to organic C = O and C-OH bonds (Di et al., 2024; Keerthana et al., 2024). Furthermore, the deconvoluted N1 s spectrum (Fig. 6 (d)) exhibited binding energies at 399 and 400.47 eV, indicative of the presence of pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen (Deng et al., 2023). These findings from FTIR and XPS analyses indicated the chemical modification of the TW-CDs surface by oxygen-rich groups, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl, as well as sp2 hybridized aromatic fragments. The presence of these oxygen-rich functional groups suggests that the TW-CDs are highly hydrophilic, which is advantageous for advanced studies on their toxicological assessment. Furthermore, the investigation of the zeta potential indicated that the surface of the TW-CDs exhibited a negative polarity, with a zeta potential value of −18.2 mV, signifying high stability. The negative zeta potential values are ascribed to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl, carbonyl, and amine, on the surface of the TW-CDs. These hydrophilic groups significantly enhance the solubility and sustainability of the TW-CDs in aqueous media, thereby facilitating their utilization in biochemical and biomedical applications. Overall, the comprehensive characterization of the TW-CDs through XPS, FTIR, and zeta potential analysis provides valuable insights into their surface chemistry, stability, and potential applications in various fields, including biomedicine and biochemistry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect and the mechanism of anti-microbial activity
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial efficacy of synthesized TW-CDs was investigated through in vitro testing against E. coli and B. subtilis. The results indicated a notable antibacterial activity of the prepared TW-CDs against both B. subtilis, evidenced by the observation of a 12 mm inhibition zone and E. coli, with an inhibition zone of 11 mm. These findings were corroborated through the implementation of the broth microdilution method, further affirming the selective antibacterial properties of TW-CDs on both B. subtilis and E. Coli. This study underscores the potential application of TW-CDs as antimicrobial agents, particularly in the context of gram-positive as well as gram-negative bacteria.

The antibacterial activity of TW-CDs is attributed to their unique physicochemical properties, including a negative zeta potential and the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl, along with sp2 hybridized aromatic fragments (Travlou et al., 2018). These features play a pivotal role in the antibacterial mechanism of TW-CDs. The oxygen-rich functional groups contribute to the generation of active oxygen species, such as superoxide ions (O2 −), through redox reactions, inducing oxidative stress and causing damage to nucleotides, lipids, proteins, and metabolites within bacterial cells, ultimately leading to cell death. Additionally, the hydroxyl groups present on the TW-CDs surface may facilitate hydrogen bonding interactions, influencing the stability of bacterial membranes, while the sp2 hybridized aromatic fragments likely contribute to membrane disruption and increased permeability (Meziani et al., 2016).

The observed marginally stronger antibacterial activity against B. subtilis compared to E. coli aligns with previous studies suggesting variations in cell wall composition and surface charge between Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Stanković et al., 2018). The simpler and negatively charged cell surface of B. subtilis, characterized by the presence of teichoic acids on the peptidoglycan layer, may enhance the electrostatic interactions with the negatively charged TW-CDs, leading to improved penetration into the cell plasma membrane. In contrast, the more complex cell surface structure of E. coli, with a thin peptidoglycan layer and lipopolysaccharides in the outer membrane, may contribute to subtle differences in the antibacterial effects. Moreover, the antibacterial activity of TW-CDs against E. coli and B. subtilis is mediated by their physicochemical properties, including the negative zeta potential and the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups and sp2 hybridized aromatic fragments. These features enable CDs to induce oxidative stress, disrupt bacterial membranes, and ultimately lead to cell death. The observed differences in antibacterial activity between the two bacterial strains can be related to changes in the composition of their cell walls and the surface charge of their cells.

In summary, the influence of holding time and temperature on HTC of factory tea waste to convert it into solid fuel was studied. The optimal hydrochar was obtained at 220 °C and 55 min. The maximum HHV was found to be 22.26±1.10 MJ/kg. The combustion behavior and kinetics of the TW-Hydrochar were studied. Ea for TW-hydrochar showed that its combustion process was consistent with the typical Ea range observed for coal. Further, the study demonstrated the simultaneous generation of TW-CDs during the production of TW-hydrochar. The generated CDs with an average diameter of 6.5 nm with a negative zeta potential of −18.12 eV evident the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl, along with sp2 hybridized aromatic fragments which showed excelled antimicrobial activity against E. coli and B. subtilis. Moreover, the optimized HTC process showcases its potential in transforming biomass into a biofuel-rich hydrochar with properties comparable to lignite coal. The dual benefits of enhanced biofuel potential and the generation of functionalized carbon dots highlight the multifaceted applications of hydrothermal carbonization in biomass utilization and environmental remediation.
